1: The guilt offering is most holy. The asham — the offering for sacrilege and restitution — holds the highest sanctity. Isaiah 53:10 declares the Servant's death an asham: 'when you make his life a guilt offering.' Christ's sacrifice is specifically an asham: it not only atones (chatat function) but restores the surplus owed (chomesh function), providing the overflow of righteousness credited to believers (Romans 5:17; 2 Corinthians 5:21).
2: The asham's blood is thrown (zeriqah) against the altar sides — the reparation gesture distinguishing the asham from the chatat's horn-application. Christ's blood was shed publicly, visibly, thoroughly: 'whom God put forward as a propitiation by his blood' (Romans 3:25). The public blood-throwing of the asham is the type of the public display of Christ's sacrifice — 'Jesus Christ was publicly portrayed as crucified' (Galatians 3:1, proegraphe, openly depicted/posted).
3: All the fat portions burned — the richness of the sacrificial animal belongs entirely to YHWH. The fat is YHWH's proprietary portion across all sacrifice types. Paul: 'in him all the fullness (pleroma) of deity dwells bodily' (Colossians 2:9). Christ is the one in whom the divine 'richness' — the full weight of YHWH's glory — dwells; his offering to the Father is not the leanness but the fullness of his divine-human personhood.
4: The kidneys and liver dedicated to YHWH — the seat of intellect and will given entirely over. Christ's prayer in Gethsemane is the ultimate surrender of the will: 'Not my will, but yours be done' (Luke 22:42). The organs associated with discernment and resolve in Hebrew anthropology are the very capacities Christ surrenders to the Father's will in the garden, fulfilling what every guilt-offering only signified — the complete handing over of the innermost self to God.
5: The fat portions burned as a food offering — YHWH's portion of the asham. Christ's life offered to the Father is a 'fragrant offering and sacrifice to God' (Ephesians 5:2: prosphoran kai thusian to theo eis osme euodias). The burning fat's ascending aroma is the type of the sweet-savor offering that Christ's entire life, from incarnation to resurrection, presents to the Father.
6: Male priests only eat the asham in the sanctuary. The priestly access to the guilt offering's meat is the type of the believer's access to Christ's righteousness: 'Since we have a great high priest who has passed through the heavens, Jesus the Son of God... let us draw near with confidence to the throne of grace' (Hebrews 4:14,16). The most-holy-space restriction of the type gives way to the boldness of access that the antitype enables.
7: The guilt offering and the sin offering share the same law; the atoning priest has the offering. The priest who makes atonement personally receives the offering's meat. Christ who made atonement receives the reward of his suffering: 'he shall see his offspring; he shall prolong his days... he shall see the fruit of the travail of his soul and be satisfied' (Isaiah 53:10-11). The priest's portion is the type of Christ's 'portion' — the people for whom he atoned, now given to him as his own (John 17:24).
8: The officiating priest keeps the burnt offering's skin — the outer garment of the sacrifice. Christ's seamless robe (John 19:23-24) was not torn but assigned by lot; even the soldiers unconsciously honored the principle that the offering's covering belongs to the one who performs the sacrificial act. The outer garment preserved in the burnt offering type is fulfilled in the seamless robe preserved at the cross.
9: The officiating priest keeps the grain offerings — prepared offerings (oven, pan, griddle) belong personally to the performing priest. The priest who serves personally at the altar receives the fruits of the offering. Christ's priestly reward is personal: 'the joy set before him' (Hebrews 12:2) for which he endured the cross, and the people who will forever worship him — his inheritance (Psalms 2:8; Hebrews 2:13: 'Behold, I and the children God has given me').
10: Raw/dry grain offerings shared equally among all Aaron's sons — the communal distribution. Christ's priestly gifts extend to all his people equally: the Spirit poured out 'on all flesh' (Acts 2:17), distributed 'to each one individually as he wills' (1 Corinthians 12:11). The equal distribution of certain priestly portions is the type of the Spirit's equal-access gifts given to the entire new-covenant priesthood.
11: The law of the peace offering — the shelamim, the fellowship/communion offering in which YHWH, priest, and worshiper all receive portions. The peace offering is the most comprehensive type of the Lord's Supper: the shared meal in which God, mediator, and community together feast. Paul: 'The cup of blessing that we bless, is it not a participation (koinonia) in the blood of Christ? The bread that we break, is it not a participation in the body of Christ?' (1 Corinthians 10:16). The shelamim's three-way shared meal is the type of eucharistic koinonia.
12: The toda (thanksgiving) peace offering with its elaborate bread accompaniment. The toda is the most festive subcategory, requiring the most bread and eaten on the strictest one-day deadline. The Lord's Supper is specifically a toda in character: 'he took bread, and when he had given thanks (eucharistesas)...' (Luke 22:19; 1 Corinthians 11:24 — eucharistia = thanksgiving = toda). The entire eucharistic tradition is named after the thanksgiving dimension of the toda-shelamim.
13: Leavened bread with the toda — the only use of leaven in a sacrificial context. The toda's leavened bread marks it as the most this-worldly, creaturely offering: the product of fermentation, of real time and ordinary kitchen life, is brought into the presence of YHWH alongside the unleavened. The Supper uses ordinary bread and wine — the creaturely, fermented produce of vineyard and grain field — as the vehicle of Christ's body and blood. The toda's leavened bread is the type of the ordinary elements elevated to extraordinary use.
14: One bread from each type given as terumah to the officiating priest. The priest receives the firstfruit of the toda's bread as his portion. Christ receives the 'firstfruit' of the redeemed community — 'the firstborn among many brothers' (Romans 8:29); the new community born from his todah-sacrifice is his priestly portion, the first of the harvest his resurrection began.
15: The toda must be eaten on the day of offering — no overnight remainder. The urgent one-day window ensures that the thanksgiving feast is a genuine communal celebration, not a food store. The Lord's Supper is similarly an urgent present reality, not a deposit for future use: 'as often as you eat this bread and drink the cup, you proclaim the Lord's death until he comes' (1 Corinthians 11:26). The eschatological 'until he comes' creates the Supper's own temporal urgency.
16: Vow and freewill peace offerings have a two-day window. The less ceremonially charged types (neder and nadavah) permit a more relaxed eating schedule. The variety of peace offering types — toda (most urgent), neder/nadavah (more leisurely) — suggests that the fellowship-meal with YHWH takes varied forms appropriate to the occasion. The Lord's Supper serves both the urgent communal assembly and the more personal devotional settings that the 'breaking of bread from house to house' (Acts 2:46) represents.
17: Third-day meat must be burned — the third day marks the boundary between valid flesh and corrupted. The 'third day' is the decisive boundary in Levitical sacrifice: what is not consumed by then is tainted. Christ's body does not cross the third-day boundary into corruption: 'You will not abandon my soul to Sheol, or let your holy one see corruption' (Psalms 16:10 cited in Acts 2:27,31; Leviticus 13:34-35). The third-day resurrection is the answer to the third-day corruption deadline — the body of Christ does not become pigul.
18: Pigul yihyeh — it shall be tainted. Third-day meat is void and harmful to eat. The one who eats pigul bears his own iniquity. Paul warns against eating the Lord's Supper 'in an unworthy manner': 'whoever eats the bread or drinks the cup of the Lord in an unworthy manner will be guilty concerning the body and blood of the Lord... eats and drinks judgment on himself' (1 Corinthians 11:27,29). The pigul warning — eating void/tainted sacrifice incurs personal guilt — is the type of the Supper-worthiness requirement.
19: Flesh touching any unclean thing must be burned, not eaten; only clean persons may eat the shelamim. The peace offering's cleanliness requirements — protecting the sacred communal meal from contamination — point to Paul's instruction: 'Let a person examine himself, then, and so eat of the bread and drink of the cup' (1 Corinthians 11:28). Self-examination before communion is the new covenant application of the clean-person-only requirement of the shelamim.
20: Eating the shelamim while personally impure triggers karet — the most severe penalty. The Lord's Supper eaten in an unworthy manner brings judgment (1 Corinthians 11:29-30: 'many of you are weak and ill, and some have died'). The severity of the shelamim's impurity-violation penalty is carried forward into the apostolic warning about the Supper's sacred character; both reflect the seriousness of profaning the primary fellowship-meal between YHWH/Christ and his people.
21: Any impurity source — human, animal, insect — disqualifies from shelamim eating. The comprehensive coverage of all impurity sources matches the comprehensive scope of the self-examination required before communion. The point is not that one must be perfect but that one must have dealt with known sin, sought reconciliation, and come to the table with genuine faith rather than careless familiarity.
22: The fat prohibition legislation formally introduced. The fat — the richness of the animal — belongs exclusively to YHWH across all sacrifice contexts. The proprietary claim of the Creator on the 'richness' of creation points to the doxological principle: 'to him who is able to do far more abundantly than all that we ask or think, to him be glory... forever' (Ephesians 3:20-21). The fat that burns on the altar and ascends as aroma to YHWH is the type of all creaturely excellence rendered back to its source.
23: No fat of ox, sheep, or goat shall be eaten — the restriction to sacrificial species. The proprietary claim applies specifically to animals that participate in the sanctuary system. Everything that belongs to the covenant worship economy ultimately belongs to YHWH. Christ's sacrifice returns to the Father the 'fat' of perfect humanity — the fullness of what human nature was meant to be and offer to God (Hebrews 10:5-7: 'A body you have prepared for me... I have come to do your will, O God').
24: Neveilah and terefah fat may be used for other purposes but not eaten. Even the fat of animals outside the sacrificial system is not available for human consumption. The principle of divine priority on the richness of creation is comprehensive even beyond the sanctuary context. Christ's active obedience covers the full scope of human failure to honor this priority: in his life, the fat — the fullness of human flourishing — is offered wholly to the Father without reservation.
25: Eating the sacrificial animals' fat triggers karet. The severity of the fat-prohibition's penalty confirms that the fat is not merely ritually significant but represents YHWH's proprietary claim on the highest portion of his creatures' lives. To consume it is to steal what belongs to God. Ananias and Sapphira's death (Acts 5:1-11) — keeping back a portion of what was devoted to God — is the new covenant narrative instantiation of this principle.
26: The blood prohibition extended to all locations, all animals, all people. The universal, comprehensive scope of the blood-prohibition — no exceptions for location, species, or social status — anticipates the universal scope of Christ's atoning blood: 'the blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin' (1 John 1:7). The blood that belongs to YHWH because it is the nefesh of the animal is the type of Christ's blood, which is the nefesh — the life — of the Son of God given for the life of the world (John 6:51).
27: Karet for blood consumption. The universal death-penalty for blood-eating confirms that the blood-as-nefesh principle is not decorative but constitutive of the entire atonement system. Christ's blood is the only blood that can be 'consumed' in a legitimate sense — not as food (the prohibition stands) but as the offered life that covers human death: 'unless you eat the flesh of the Son of Man and drink his blood, you have no life in you' (John 6:53). The metaphorical drinking of Christ's blood — receiving his life-given-in-death — is not a violation but the fulfillment of what the blood-prohibition pointed toward.
28: The wave offering legislation formally introduced. The waving gesture — presenting to YHWH and receiving back — is the physical enactment of the consecration-and-return dynamic at the heart of all sacrificial theology. Christ's offering to the Father and the Father's reception-and-return of him in resurrection is the ultimate tenupha: 'God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it' (Acts 2:24). The wave-and-return is the resurrection pattern enacted in priestly miniature.
29: The offerer personally brings the offerings — the priestly ceremony begins with the offerer's personal presentation. The believer's personal presentation of self to God — 'present your bodies as a living sacrifice, holy and acceptable to God' (Romans 12:1) — is the new covenant fulfillment of the offerer's personal presentation of the peace offering. The shelamim's personal-involvement requirement is transformed into the entire-life offering of the new covenant worshiper.
30: The offerer's own hands bring the breast and fat as the wave offering. The offerer personally waves the offering before YHWH — the most intimate participation in the sacrificial act available to a non-priest. Christ the offerer and the offering presents himself: 'who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God' (Hebrews 9:14). The agent and the object of the offering are the same person; the wave is self-presentation to the Father.
31: The fat burns on the altar (YHWH's portion); the breast becomes the shared priestly portion. The three-way division — YHWH (fat), all priests (breast), officiating priest (thigh) — models the structure of the eschatological feast in which God, the mediating Son, and the priestly people all participate. The Lord's Supper anticipates the Marriage Supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:9) in which all three parties — Father, Christ, and redeemed community — will feast together.
32: The right thigh as contribution (terumah) to the officiating priest — the most honorable portion to the priest who performed the blood manipulation. Christ receives the highest honor from the Father for performing the greatest priestly act: 'God has highly exalted him and bestowed on him the name that is above every name' (Philippians 2:9). The right-side priority of the heave thigh is the type of the resurrection-honor: 'he sat down at the right hand of the Majesty on high' (Hebrews 1:3).
33: The priest who manipulates the blood receives the right thigh. The blood-manipulation as the critical priestly act that earns the most honorable portion — this links directly to Christ: his blood-shedding is the definitive priestly act (Hebrews 9:12: 'he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood') that earns the highest honor.
34: YHWH has taken the breast and thigh from Israel and given them to the priests as a perpetual due. The divine taking-and-giving of the priestly portions is the type of the divine taking-and-giving in Christ's sacrifice: the Father 'gave' his Son (John 3:16), took him through death, and gave him back in resurrection as 'the firstborn from the dead' (Colossians 1:18; Revelation 1:5). The priestly prebend grounded in divine redistribution is the type of the resurrection as divine vindication.
35: The priestly portions are called mishchat aharon umishchat banav — the portion of Aaron and his sons from the day of their anointing. Christ's priestly portion — the people given to him — is grounded in his anointing: the baptismal voice (Matthew 3:17), the Spirit descending (Matthew 3:16), the Father's declaration. The priestly portion is anointing-based, not merit-based. Christ receives the nations as his inheritance (Psalms 2:8) from the day of his messianic anointing.
36: The perpetual due throughout generations — chok-olam ledorotam. Christ's priestly portion is eternal: 'They will be his people, and God himself will be with them as their God' (Revelation 21:3). The permanent priestly inheritance is the type of the eternal covenant people who will dwell with Christ forever. The Levitical perpetuity formula reaches its ultimate fulfillment in the new creation where the priestly people and the eternal Priest share the divine presence without end.
37: The closing summary names six sacrifice types: olah, minchah, chatat, asham, millu'im, shelamim. All six are fulfilled in Christ: burnt offering (total self-consecration), grain offering (consecrated human life), sin offering (bearing human sin), guilt offering (restoring what was owed), ordination offering (the once-for-all ordination of the eternal Priest), and peace offering (the fellowship meal restored between God and humanity). The Levitical sacrifice system in its totality is the multi-faceted anticipation of the one sacrifice of Christ whose single act fulfills every type simultaneously.
38: All six sacrifice rubrics originate at Sinai, commanded on the day Israel was told to bring offerings in the wilderness. The Sinaitic origin formula grounds every sacrifice in the moment of covenant formation. Christ's sacrifice was not an improvised response to human sin but was foreordained before the foundation of the world (1 Peter 1:19-20: 'a lamb without blemish or spot who was foreknown before the foundation of the world') — the eternal purpose behind which the Sinai legislation was a temporal inscription.
